title: 25 Tahsas — St. Ganna (The Nativity of Christ) & The Assembly
narrative:
The twenty-fifth day of Tahsas is the feast of Ganna — the Ethiopian name for the Nativity of the Lord Jesus Christ — and it is the theological summit not only of this month but of the entire winter liturgical cycle, the event toward which all the prophets pointed, all the martyrs bore witness, and all the holy women and men whose memories have filled the preceding twenty-four days have been preparing the heart to receive. In the fullness of time, when the long night of human exile from God had stretched through all the generations from Adam to Augustus, the eternal Word of the Father, by whom all things were made, was born of the Virgin Mary, wrapped in swaddling cloths, and laid in a manger in a cave outside the city of Bethlehem of Judea. The angels sang; the shepherds came running; the stars moved; the wise men were set in motion by a light in the sky; and the ancient groan of creation was answered by the cry of an infant — for this cry was the voice of the one who had called creation into being, now calling to creation from within its own depths. The Ethiopian faithful celebrate this feast with a great eucharistic liturgy followed by the traditional game of Ganna, a form of field hockey that in the popular tradition was played by shepherds on the night of the Lord's birth.

Saint Tekle Haymanot, the great fourteenth-century Ethiopian monastic saint whose monthly commemoration falls on this day, was himself a kind of living icon of the Incarnation's effect upon humanity: born in Shewa, trained in the ancient monasteries of Debre Damo, he preached the Gospel throughout Ethiopia, founded the monastery of Debre Libanos which became the spiritual heart of the Ethiopian Church, and in the end stood in prayer upon one leg for seven years until the other leg fell away — an extreme of mortification that the tradition reads as the body itself being transformed into an instrument of the spirit's unbroken attention to God. His name in the Ge'ez means 'Plant of the Holy Trinity,' and he was indeed a plant that had put its roots deep into the soil of the Incarnate God and brought forth fruit a hundredfold.

Saint Adai the Apostle, venerated in the Syriac and Ethiopian traditions as the apostle of Edessa and Mesopotamia, completes the assembly of this supreme day. Adai was one of the seventy disciples of the Lord and was dispatched by the Apostle Thomas to bring healing and the Gospel to King Abgar V of Edessa, who had written to Jesus requesting a cure for his illness; the tradition records that Adai brought with him the miraculous Image of Edessa — the holy face of Christ imprinted upon cloth — and that the sight of this image brought both healing and conversion to the king and the city. Adai's mission to the edges of the Semitic world declares on this feast day of the Incarnation that the light born in Bethlehem is not confined to one nation or one language but is sent to all peoples, all tongues, all the distant cities of human habitation.

The theological seal of this twenty-fifth day of Tahsas — the day of Ganna — is the mystery that surpasses all other mysteries and is the seal of all the days that have preceded it: the mystery of the Incarnation itself, of God become man without ceasing to be God, of the infinite become finite without diminishing His infinity, of the immortal become mortal without losing His immortality. The Word who spoke light into darkness has become a child who must learn to speak; the one through whom the worlds were made has been made in the womb of a creature; the Ancient of Days lies in the manger of a cave, young beyond all youth, that the old Adam might be made new. The Church falls silent before this mystery, knowing that no word of hers, however beautiful, can contain it; she can only receive it on her knees, with the shepherds, with Tekle Haymanot, with Adai, with all the saints whose memory has filled this holy month — and sing with the angels the one song that is adequate to the occasion: Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace, goodwill toward men.
